items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What sport is best suited to type I (slow-twitch) fibres?
items.why: Students confuse which fibres suit which event. Type I = slow, fatigue-resistant, aerobic (endurance). Type II = fast, powerful, anaerobic (sprint/power). Distance runners are type-I-dominant.
The answer examiners want is: SPORT. “FITT” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Tapering. “Overload” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters in SPORT principle?
items.why: The real figure is 5 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
